schools.summary: That the child should be a Nazarite to God from the womb and should begin to deliver Israel: 'for, lo, thou shalt conceive, and bear a son; and no razor shall come on his head: for the child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb: and he shall begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines' — the consecration and the partial deliverance — 'for the child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb: and he shall begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines': Samson is set apart to God from birth and commissioned only to begin the deliverance (the witnesses noting the significance — the child marked out for God from the womb, a Nazarite not for a season, as the usual vow, but for life, the uncut hair the sign of his consecration and the seat of his strength, his whole life dedicated to God's service from the first, and his commission expressly to begin, not to complete, the deliverance from the Philistines, the work he opened that David would finish, the lifelong Nazariteship the mark of a man wholly God's, the begin to deliver showing the partial and preparatory nature of his work, the deliverer raised up in God's time and measure, the lesson — that God consecrates his servants to himself, sometimes from the womb, setting them apart for the work he has appointed, 'before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee... debates.question: What does the child being a Nazarite from the womb who shall begin to deliver Israel signify?

sides.position: The man consecrated to God from his birth for a divine work, and the deliverance God begins in his appointed time and way - the child set apart for life with the uncut hair the sign of his consecration and seat of his strength, commissioned to begin not complete the deliverance, God consecrating his servants from the womb (Jeremiah 1:5), his deliverances coming by stages (1 Corinthians 3:6), a type of the greater Deliverer holy from the womb (Luke 1:35)

the angel turning back Manoah's question, declining to give a name to be comprehended, marking himself as more than a creature (the name past finding out, Psalm 139:6) — the answer (why askest thou after my name); 'seeing it is secret?' (the name 'secret' being the same word as 'Wonderful,' the Angel of the covenant declaring a name no creature bears, a manifestation of the Son of God before his incarnation, his person beyond comprehension, the name Wonderful, Isaiah 9:6; John 1:18) — the figure (the divine messenger whose secret name reveals his Godhead, the providence, the name, the angel ascending in the flame to follow, vv.19-20) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the angel's secret name signify?

sides.position: The divine messenger of the covenant, whose secret and wonderful name marks him as more than a creature, a manifestation of God himself - the word 'secret' the same as 'Wonderful' in Isaiah's name for Messiah, the Angel of the covenant declaring a name no creature bears, the pre-incarnate Christ bearing a name past finding out (Isaiah 9:6), the being of God beyond comprehension (Psalm 139:6), the Angel of the LORD who is himself the LORD (John 1:18)

themes: And the angel of the LORD said unto him, Why askest thou thus after my name, seeing it is secret?
The angel's answer, that his name is secret, or Wonderful (the crux of the divine messenger of the covenant, whose secret and wonderful name marks him as more than a creature)

the word 'secret' being the same as 'Wonderful' in Isaiah's name for Messiah, the angel declaring his name past finding out, marking him as no created angel but the Angel of the covenant, a manifestation of the Son of God before his incarnation
the pre-incarnate Christ bearing a name past finding out, the Angel of the LORD who is himself the LORD, fulfilled in Christ the Word made flesh (Isaiah 9:6; Psalm 139:6; John 1:18)

schools.summary: Manoah offering the kid to the LORD, and the angel doing wondrously: 'so Manoah took a kid with a meat offering, and offered it upon a rock unto the LORD: and the angel did wondrously; and Manoah and his wife looked on' — Manoah offered to the LORD, and the angel worked a wonder (the so Manoah took a kid with a meat offering, and offered it upon a rock unto the LORD, the offering, the and the angel did wondrously; and Manoah and his wife looked on, the wonder, the figure, the offering accepted and the wonder wrought, the divine messenger confirming his nature, the truth that the angel did wondrously, confirming his Godhead before their eyes, Judges 13:18, the figure of the wonder that reveals the divine, the offering owned of God, the offering of Manoah, the wonder wrought, vv.18); Manoah offering to the LORD (the offering) — the offering (a kid unto the LORD); 'the angel did wondrously; and Manoah and his wife looked on' (the angel working a wonder before them, the marvel, Judges 13:18, the figure, the wonder that reveals the divine, the providence, the wonder, the angel ascending in the flame to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: So Manoah took a kid with a meat offering, and offered it upon a rock unto the LORD: and the angel did wondrously; and Manoah and his wife looked on.
Manoah offering the kid to the LORD, and the angel doing wondrously
Manoah offering to the LORD, and the angel working a wonder before their eyes, confirming his divine nature
the wonder that reveals the divine (Judges 13:18)

schools.summary: The angel of the LORD ascending in the flame of the altar, at which Manoah and his wife fell on their faces: 'for it came to pass, when the flame went up toward heaven from off the altar, that the angel of the LORD ascended in the flame of the altar. And Manoah and his wife looked on it, and fell on their faces to the ground' — the manifestation of the divine in the sacrifice — 'for it came to pass, when the flame went up toward heaven from off the altar, that the angel of the LORD ascended in the flame of the altar. And Manoah and his wife looked on it, and fell on their faces to the ground': the angel ascends in the flame, revealing his Godhead, and the pair fall in worship (the witnesses noting the significance — the crowning wonder by which the Angel of the LORD revealed himself as divine, ascending heavenward in the very flame of the burnt offering, identifying himself with the sacrifice that rose to God, so that Manoah and his wife, beholding, fell prostrate in awe and worship, knowing now that they had seen no mere man but the LORD, the angel in the flame the seal of his deity, the divine messenger one with the offering, the worshippers cast to the ground in holy fear, the lesson — that God reveals himself in the sacrifice, the Angel of the covenant ascending in the flame a figure of the acceptance of the offering and the presence of God in it, 'by him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually,' Hebrews 13:15, the truth that the manifestation of God brings the creature low in worship, the right response to the divine presence being to fall on the face, 'and when I saw him, I fell at his feet as dead,' Revelation 1:17, the figure — the Angel of the LORD identifying himself with the burnt offering and ascending to God, fulfilled in Christ who is both the offering and the one who ascended, 'Christ also hath loved us, and hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God,' Ephesians 5:2, the truth that the wonder revealed the deity of the Angel and drew forth the worship due to God alone, the figure of the divine self-revelation in the sacrifice, the Angel who is one with the offering ascending to God and revealing his Godhead, the call to fall before the God who reveals himself in the sacrifice, the assurance that the Angel of the covenant is himself the God who accepts the offering, the figure of the divine self-revelation in the sacrifice, the Angel who is one with the offering ascending to God and revealing his Godhead); the angel ascending heavenward in the flame of the burnt offering, identifying himself with the sacrifice that rose to God, the seal of his deity (the divine in the sacrifice, Hebrews 13:15) — the ascent (in the flame of the altar); 'And Manoah and his wife looked on it, and fell on their faces to the ground' (Manoah and his wife falling prostrate in awe and worship, knowing they had seen the LORD, the manifestation of God bringing the creature low, the worship before the divine, Revelation 1:17; Ephesians 5:2) — the figure (the Angel of the LORD identifying himself with the burnt offering and ascending to God, the providence, the worship, Manoah's fear of death to follow, vv.21-22) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the angel ascending in the flame of the altar reveal?

sides.position: The divine self-revelation in the sacrifice, the Angel who is one with the offering ascending to God and revealing his Godhead - the crowning wonder by which the Angel revealed himself divine, identifying himself with the burnt offering that rose to God, drawing the worshippers prostrate, God revealing himself in the sacrifice (Hebrews 13:15), the manifestation bringing the creature low (Revelation 1:17), the Angel fulfilled in Christ both offering and ascended (Ephesians 5:2)

themes: For it came to pass, when the flame went up toward heaven from off the altar, that the angel of the LORD ascended in the flame of the altar. And Manoah and his wife looked on it, and fell on their faces to the ground.
The angel of the LORD ascending in the flame of the altar, at which Manoah and his wife fell on their faces (the crux of the divine self-revelation in the sacrifice)

the crowning wonder by which the Angel revealed himself divine, ascending in the very flame of the burnt offering, identifying himself with the sacrifice that rose to God, so that the pair fell prostrate knowing they had seen the LORD

God revealing himself in the sacrifice, the manifestation of God bringing the creature low in worship, the Angel one with the offering, fulfilled in Christ who is both offering and the one who ascended (Hebrews 13:15; Revelation 1:17; Ephesians 5:2)

schools.summary: The angel appearing no more, and Manoah knowing he was the angel of the LORD: 'but the angel of the LORD did no more appear to Manoah and to his wife. Then Manoah knew that he was an angel of the LORD' — the angel vanished, and Manoah recognized the divine messenger (the but the angel of the LORD did no more appear to Manoah and to his wife, the angel vanished, the then Manoah knew that he was an angel of the LORD, the recognition, the figure, the messenger withdrawn and the truth perceived, the divine nature owned, the truth that Manoah now knew the visitant was the angel of the LORD, Judges 6:22, the figure of the recognition of the divine after the wonder, the messenger owned, the vanishing of the angel, the recognition, vv.20); the angel appearing no more (the withdrawal, Judges 6:22) — the vanishing (no more appear); 'Then Manoah knew that he was an angel of the LORD' (Manoah's clear recognition of the divine messenger, the truth owned, the figure, the recognition of the divine after the wonder, the providence, the recognition, Manoah's fear of death to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But the angel of the LORD did no more appear to Manoah and to his wife. Then Manoah knew that he was an angel of the LORD.
The angel appearing no more, and Manoah knowing he was the angel of the LORD
the angel vanishing and Manoah recognizing the divine messenger
the truth owned after the wonder (Judges 6:22)

schools.summary: Manoah's fear that they must die because they had seen God: 'and Manoah said unto his wife, We shall surely die, because we have seen God' — the fear of death at seeing God — 'and Manoah said unto his wife, We shall surely die, because we have seen God': Manoah, recognizing the divine visitant, fears that the sight of God means death (the witnesses noting the significance — the deep conviction, grounded in the word and the conscience of fallen man, that to see God is to die, the sinner's awe before the holy presence he cannot bear, Manoah expressing the universal sense that no creature, least of all a sinner, can behold the unveiled God and live, the fear not unbelief but reverence, the conviction that the gulf between the holy God and sinful man is mortal, the sight of God a thing too great for flesh, the lesson — that sinful man cannot stand before the holy God in his own strength, the manifestation of God striking terror into the conscience that knows its guilt, 'there shall no man see me, and live,' Exodus 33:20, the truth that the awe and dread of God's presence is the right instinct of the creature, deepened in the sinner by the knowledge of his sin, 'who is able to stand before this holy LORD God?' 1 Samuel 6:20, the figure — the terror of the unveiled God before which flesh must perish, answered only in the Mediator through whom we may draw near, 'but ye are come unto mount Sion... and to Jesus the mediator,' Hebrews 12:22-24, the truth that the fear of death at seeing God points to the need of a Mediator in whom alone we may see God and live, the figure of the awe of sinful man before the manifested God, the conviction that no man can see God and live, the call to draw near to God only through the Mediator, the assurance that in Christ we may behold God and not die, the figure of the awe of sinful man before the manifested God, and the conviction that no man can see God and live); Manoah turning to his wife in fear, recognizing the divine visitant and dreading the consequence (the fear of the divine presence, 1 Samuel 6:20) — the word (Manoah to his wife); 'We shall surely die, because we have seen God' (Manoah's conviction that to see God is to die, the sinner's awe before the holy presence, the gulf between the holy God and sinful man, answered only in the Mediator, no man can see God and live, Exodus 33:20; Hebrews 12:22-24) — the figure (the terror of the unveiled God before which flesh must perish, the providence, the fear, the wife's faith answering to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Manoah's fear 'we shall surely die, because we have seen God' express?

sides.position: The awe of sinful man before the manifested God, the conviction that no man can see God and live - the deep conviction grounded in word and conscience that to see God is to die, the sinner's awe before the holy presence, the fear not unbelief but reverence, sinful man unable to stand before the holy God (Exodus 33:20; 1 Samuel 6:20), answered only in the Mediator through whom we may draw near (Hebrews 12:22-24)

themes: And Manoah said unto his wife, We shall surely die, because we have seen God.
Manoah's fear that they must die because they had seen God (the crux of the awe of sinful man before the manifested God)
the deep conviction, grounded in the word and conscience, that to see God is to die, the sinner's awe before the holy presence he cannot bear, the fear not unbelief but reverence
sinful man unable to stand before the holy God in his own strength, answered only in the Mediator through whom we may draw near (Exodus 33:20; 1 Samuel 6:20; Hebrews 12:22-24)

schools.summary: The wife's faith, reasoning from God's gracious dealings that he did not mean to slay them: 'but his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands, neither would he have shewed us all these things, nor would as at this time have told us such things as these' — the faith that reasons from grace — 'but his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands, neither would he have shewed us all these things': the wife answers her husband's fear with a sound argument of faith (the witnesses noting the significance — the calm and believing reasoning of the woman, who, stronger in faith than her husband, gathers from the very tokens of God's favour, the accepted offering, the revelation, the gracious promise, that he did not purpose their death but their good, drawing from God's gracious dealings the assurance of his gracious intent, the faith that argues from the evidences of God's kindness to the certainty of his mercy, the accepted sacrifice a pledge of acceptance, the promise of a son a pledge of life, the revelation a sign of favour not of doom, the lesson — that faith reasons rightly from the gifts and dealings of God to his good purpose, taking the tokens of his grace as assurance of his favour, 'he that spared not his own Son... how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?' Romans 8:32, the truth that God's past mercies are the ground of present confidence, the accepted offering and the given promise pledges of his good will, the figure — the believing soul that draws assurance from the evidences of grace, the accepted sacrifice the pledge that God means life and not death, fulfilled in the assurance the believer has through the accepted offering of Christ, 'being now justified by his blood, we shall be saved from wrath through him,' Romans 5:9, the truth that the calm reasoning of faith stills the fears that the sense of guilt awakens, the figure of the faith that reasons from the tokens of God's grace to the assurance of his good purpose, the call to reason from God's mercies to confidence in his love, the assurance that the accepted offering of Christ is the pledge of our salvation, the figure of the faith that reasons from the tokens of God's grace to the assurance of his good purpose); the wife reasoning that the accepted offering was a pledge that God did not mean their death, the faith stronger than her husband's (the argument from grace, Romans 8:32) — the reasoning (he would not have received the offering); 'neither would he have shewed us all these things, nor would as at this time have told us such things as these' (the wife reasoning further that the revelation and the gracious promise were tokens of favour, not of doom, the believing soul drawing assurance from the evidences of grace, fulfilled in the assurance through Christ's accepted offering, the pledge of God's good will, Romans 5:9) — the figure (the believing soul that draws assurance from the evidences of grace, the providence, the faith, the birth and growth of Samson to follow, vv.24) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the wife's reasoning teach about faith?

sides.position: The faith that reasons from the tokens of God's grace to the assurance of his good purpose - the calm believing reasoning of the woman gathering from the accepted offering, the revelation, and the promise that God purposed their good not their death, faith arguing from the evidences of God's kindness to the certainty of his mercy (Romans 8:32), the accepted sacrifice the pledge that God means life, fulfilled in the assurance through Christ's accepted offering (Romans 5:9)

themes: But his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands, neither would he have shewed us all these things, nor would as at this time have told us such things as these.
The wife's faith, reasoning from God's gracious dealings that he did not mean to slay them (the crux of the faith that reasons from the tokens of God's grace to the assurance of his good purpose)
the calm and believing reasoning of the woman, stronger in faith than her husband, gathering from the tokens of God's favour that he purposed their good and not their death

faith arguing from the evidences of God's kindness to the certainty of his mercy, God's past mercies the ground of present confidence, fulfilled in the assurance through Christ's accepted offering (Romans 8:32; Romans 5:9)

schools.summary: The birth of Samson, the child growing and blessed by the LORD: 'and the woman bare a son, and called his name Samson: and the child grew, and the LORD blessed him' — the promise was fulfilled in Samson's birth and the LORD's blessing on his growth (the and the woman bare a son, and called his name Samson, the birth, the and the child grew, and the LORD blessed him, the growth and blessing, the figure, the promise fulfilled, the child of wonder born and blessed of God, the truth that the promised son was born and the LORD blessed him, Luke 1:80, the figure of God's faithfulness in giving and blessing the child of promise, the birth of Samson, the LORD's blessing, vv.5); the birth of Samson (the fulfilment, Luke 1:80) — the birth (Samson born); 'the child grew, and the LORD blessed him' (the child growing under God's blessing, the favour, the figure, God's faithfulness in giving and blessing the child of promise, the providence, the blessing, the Spirit beginning to move him to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the woman bare a son, and called his name Samson: and the child grew, and the LORD blessed him.
The birth of Samson, the child growing and blessed by the LORD
the promise fulfilled in Samson's birth and the LORD's blessing on his growth
God's faithfulness in the child of promise (Luke 1:80)

schools.summary: The Spirit of the LORD beginning to move Samson in the camp of Dan: 'and the Spirit of the LORD began to move him at times in the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol' — the Spirit began to stir Samson, fitting him for his work (the and the Spirit of the LORD began to move him at times, the Spirit's stirring, the in the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol, the place, the figure, the Spirit beginning to work in the consecrated deliverer, the gift stirring for the appointed task, the truth that the Spirit of the LORD began to move Samson for his work, Judges 14:6, the figure of the Spirit who equips and stirs God's servant for his calling, the Spirit's beginning, the deliverer stirred, vv.5); the Spirit beginning to move Samson (the stirring, Judges 14:6) — the stirring (the Spirit moved him); 'in the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol' (the place of the Spirit's first working, the locale, the figure, the Spirit who equips and stirs God's servant, the providence, the stirring, Samson's exploits to follow, ch.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the Spirit of the LORD began to move him at times in the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol.
